speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three-- we would like to continue, please, from Frankfurt am Main.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, the main-- that was where the airbase was that we were-- that we were stationed. It was also part of the Seventh Army headquarters. And-- and keep in mind, this would be like Los Angeles International Airport is today. It was just airplanes coming out your ears. Just-- it was really a dangerous place to fly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what they did was they split the squadrons up from the various groups and moved the little airplanes to satellite strips, which is what they call them-- satellite strips-- and what it would be would be a big field someplace, where they would put down the steel matting if it was necessary. And we would be-- the 16 airplanes, the pilots, and the crews would be-- that would be their base, all within 20, 30, 40 miles of Frankfort, 60 tops. One of the plays-- and this is where I met a German family that became close, and I've seen them a couple of times since the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: We continued to fly the targets of opportunity missions, air to ground support through March. And-- and it's kind of ironic. I didn't get to be-- I was trained as a fighter pilot, and I was-- and I got in several dogfights, as we called them, with other-- with German airplanes. I got-- well, I'm credited with a kill and a half, which means I got one, and I shared a kill with another one, with another pilot. So in the Air Force they give you credit for a half. If you both got the guy, and they don't know who got him for sure, they give you each half. And the Navy doesn't do that. So I'm credited with a kill and a half, shooting down one and helping shoot down another Me 109.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was over Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know exactly where your target was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was-- it was in the vicinity of Schweinfurt, which was a-- a ball-- a ball bearing factory town which the Air Force had been trying to knock out for three or four years. And they would put it out of action temporarily, but they never succeeded in totally shutting it down. And I-- we-- I happened or we happened to run into some German fighters which usually wouldn't engage with us, because they didn't want to sacrifice what few airplanes they had left and what few pilots they had left in dogfights. They'd rather use those airplanes to shoot down bombers, because that's what they really were after was the bombers. But this particular instance-- I better take-- take a sip here. [DRINKS] This particular instance, we jumped these-- this gaggle of the Me 109s. They didn't see us. And we got some shots on them before they knew we were there. And we only got-- the scramble lasted less than 30 seconds, and then they were-- they got-- they left-- they left. They ran away. And-- and-- our-- and we were going to go after them. We had them cold turkey. We could have got them all. But our flight leader decided that let's not waste our ammunition on these guys. They're-- they're not worth-- they're-- we have bigger, bigger, and more important targets than these. So we just left, and when we were-- we got back to the base and were debriefed, and they used the gun camera's film of each airplane to determine, you know, what happened. You were-- they'd debrief you by-- with intelligence officers individually asking you what you saw. And then if you said, I got one, or whatever, your gun cameras had to prove it. There were no unverified kills. You couldn't say, I got 17 of them. There's a-- if your gun camera didn't show that, you didn't get any credit. That's why they had him on there. They had 8 millimeter, then later 16 millimeter cameras on-- two of them-- on-- on fighter planes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK that was-- What I was-- what I-- I think I digressed. What I was saying was I really wanted to do more dogfighting because that's what I had been trained to do the most and I thought I was good at. And of course, there's a certain mentality with fighter pilots. I don't know whether you've ever gone-- They kind of feel like they're all hot shots, you know? They wear their clothes differently. They-- they walk differently. They-- they're kind of-- they're not-- there's nothing elite about them. But they think there is. They just-- put their pants on the same way as a bomber pilot does. But-- and the bomber pilots had a great deal more responsibility than the fighter pilots did. The fighter pilots, you just had yourself to worry about and your flight, which was three other airplanes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if you-- if I was assigned to fly a wing on our flight leader, my job was to protect him. So I had to fly close to him with the two other airplanes. Whatever he did, I had to do. If we got jumped, which didn't happen very often, my job was to protect the flight leader's tail. And then I had a guy to protect my wing man and which protected my tail, and so on. But my point is, in retrospect, I'm probably a damn fool to want to-- to-- to-- to feel like I missed out on something, because that's how you get killed is a skilled pilot with better skills or more training or more guts than you or more luck than you do, and that's how 40, 50% of the fighter pilots were killed by better pilots and better airplanes. So I look back, and I think, I sure would like to have mixed it up with some more, but-- but being conservative and counting my blessings, it's-- I'm a lot better off because I didn't, because I probably would have got it. I mean, who knows. I never thought about that. I never thought about dying. I-- it was just something I-- you know, it is not going to happen to me. And-- and it didn't. So-- but I came back. But there were guys who would say, you know, jeez, this is my 47th mission, you know. I'm about due. And sometimes that would be our last mission, you know. And you'd think-- you have to pack up their stuff and send it home to their folks and write a letter, and oh it's-- But anyhow, so much for the aerial exploits of a guy who didn't get to do much of what he was trained to do the most. That's kind of the way I feel. April comes, we were 10-- 12 of us from my unit were sent from the 10th fighter group-- were sent to Frankfurt to report to Seventh Army headquarters. Nobody told us what we were going to do. We just packed our bags and-- and rode to Frankfurt on a-- on a truck. And we reported in, and we were given-- had billets-- nice, two men in a room, they-- good food. And we had been getting pretty good food, by the way, which was a whole different ballgame than in France-- terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us briefly about the difference?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes, yeah, when we were moving so much, it would be rare to get a hot meal. It would be rare. The eggs were always powdered eggs. The milk was always powdered milk. The meat was-- a lot of times was mutton or goat-- goat meat. And I couldn't handle it. I never could, and I still can't. You were eat-- you were eating-- we were moving so much that they couldn't supply us with what they normally would in an Air Corp unit-- in their Air Force unit. This is after D-day. It was just you know-- everybody was going full bore to get to Germany to end the war. So-- and then we were unfortunately-- we weren't based in one place long enough for the food guys to catch up with us where we can sit down and have mashed potatoes and chicken fried steaks and things like that-- once in a while, but very seldom. But when we got to Gelnhausen, we had our own-- just our squadron had our own mess. And we were getting food from-- eggs, for example, and vegetables, radishes. I'll never forget the first time I had a fresh radish from the farmers around there. You know, with-- our mess sergeant would go out there with a truck and a whole bunch of coffee and candy bars and what have you and come back with a truck full of fresh vegetables. And boy, I'll tell you. It was-- and we had got some venison that we killed ourselves. And it was just-- we got to eat real good, and that was such a difference, to sit down and eat a fresh egg, I mean, have two fried eggs with Spam. Spam-- everybody hated Spam. I like Spam. I still like Spam. And I buy it at the store. Spam and eggs is full of cholesterol, but I love it. OK, so that's about the food. It was-- from then on, it was good food, except-- with few exceptions, I'll say. In Frankfurt, the-- about the second day, turned out that most of these men-- and I knew some of them. My flight leader was one on them-- had been-- had flown L planes. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: L planes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the-- the one I showed you the picture of in there, the two seaters.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does the L stand for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Liason. Liaison. They call him observation-- They call them O planes now. And they still have them in the army. The army uses them. Helicopters, and they use O planes too for the same reason they use him in World War II.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does the O stand for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Observation. And you can see, you know. Up high, you can pick targets. And you can see enemy movements and things of that nature, even with the night goggles they have now, you can-- it's really amazing. I'm glad I didn't have to do all that stuff, but anyhow we were briefed that the-- that the Nazi-- and we had been-- you asked about rumors. We had-- there were rumors, even in the States. And I-- and I have a point I want to make here.

speaker: interviewer
text: In my unit here at the 70th, there were several Jewish guys from-- and these guys were from-- these were New York Jews, real-- how can I say this? Boy, they were Jews, you know, Goldberg-- and funny as hell to talk to. One-- one man who was older, he was a draftee, his wife-- and I'd say the man was probably 40 something-- had money. His wife used to come out and go see RCO all the time. Name was Captain John C. Goldbald and that ain't what we called him. But that was his name. What did you call him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gold balls. [LAUGHS] He had-- we thought the guy had cojones like that, they had to be gold. He was-- he was a lawyer, later became a federal judge. I'm still in touch with the guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his real name now?

speaker: interviewee
text: John C. Goldbald, and we called him John C.-- Jesus Christ Gold Balls is what we called him. He thinks he's Jesus Christ Gold Balls. And I don't know whether he ever-- I wrote him a letter one time after he retired, and I said, you know what-- what the guys used to call you back in 1942 in [? Cabot air? ?] And he's a federal judge now, just retired. I mean a federal judge-- that's pretty high, I guess-- a district judge, or something. And so he wrote me back. He said, I can't imagine. And I said, we used to call you Jesus Christ Gold Balls-- God Balls. And he wrote me the funniest letter with his-- on his official stationery-- the judge, you know, Supreme Court or whatever the heck it was. And he got a kick out of that. And also this he reminded me of we were having a volleyball game one time. And he and I were on the same team. And I had forgotten all about this. And you know how you rotate playing volleyball. Well, he was in front of me, and I was the second row back. And he jumped, and I went for the ball. And I hit him in the back of the head with the palm of my hand and knocked his feet totally out from under him. And then I didn't remember that. They had to go-- he was unconscious from that, plus he went-- I knocked him unconscious when I hit him. But it was an accident. I didn't-- but when he hit the ground, he was unconscious, and he hit the-- his head hit the ground again. And so the medics came, and they gave him some sniffers and checked him all over and took him away. And he was OK. He was just-- but he reminded me that, you're the guy that hit me playing volleyball and knocked me unconscious. And I-- and I said-- now, I remember that happening after he told me. But I-- but I had forgotten about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had some Jewish people in you unit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, and that was my point. Some of these guys-- this little guy's wife used to come out to the camp and see Captain Goldbald and talk about her husband, wanted to make sure he was being taken care of. That he was getting proper nourishment and, you know, was being discriminated against and all. And she was out there two or three times a week, had a car and everything, and then had a nice apartment in Corvallis. And her husband was a-- he was a-- he was trying harder than the normal guy to make it, because he had a disadvantage. He owned a business someplace in New York. He didn't have to work. He was soft hands. And he had never slept out in the mud with the mosquitoes and spiders and stuff like that, which is what they were doing. But she-- and he was a real-- we got to where we just loved this guy. He was all man, boy. And it was hard. During the-- I remember during the machine-- when the men had to crawl through the defilade, barbed wire with machine guns shooting over your head, and that-- that scared everybody, you know, because if anybody makes-- the guns are locked, so they can't come down. But they're still shooting bullets 6 inches over your head-- machine gun bullets, and some of them are tracers. And you can hear them. And I tell you what, this guy was really sweating it. But he-- when the time came for him to go, he went lickety split through there, barbed wire snags all over his face and each-- and he came out probably in the top three, time-wise, of guys that went through that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we've got a little ahead of ourselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I am, because-- but I was going to say that the mystery-- some of these Jewish men were sent to the South Pacific. And I was-- I've talked to them and talked to other guys. How come they won't let these-- I'd like to go into combat with these guys. I mean, if we're going to have to go into combat. Well, they let them go to Germany because of the way the Germans treat the Jews, which I said, well, what the-- what is that about? And nobody really knew. And I don't think it was the truth. I think it was just eeny meeny miny mo. You go to the South Pacific because they need you down there, whatever the specialty was. So-- but there were Jews in the infantry, the field artillery, the engineers, the cadet class I was in, the squadron I was in. And we didn't think anything of it, you know. I was-- a lot of them called me Chief. I didn't look like an Indian, but they knew I was Indian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a commission at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a commission, which is another-- but I got busted. I did something very naughty. And remind me to tell you. I did-- I was a first lieutenant at the time that this happened, that I went into the camps. But I did get busted to tech sergeant, which was what I was discharged as. Later, I got it back in the reserves, so it worked out. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were your orders?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, the briefings were about what we were going to do. We were going to fly specialists, medical doctors, intelligence officers, people who reported to General Eisenhower or his staff to the camps-- the Nazi concentration camps-- which they said, you know, camps. We said, camps? We didn't know anything about the-- no names at that point. And-- and it-- there would usually be flights of three to five airplanes in each group. And we would be-- there would be a flight leader. And he was really in charge of the pilots more than anything. These other men were all high-ranking officers that were our passengers-- colonels, lieutenant colonels, chief surgeons, people like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do yo remember any names that we might recognize, besides Eisenhower.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember any of them, because I never got to be around them that much. I saw Eisenhower. I saw Patton. I saw General Bradley. I saw-- but this is not like seeing you and I sitting here talking. This is-- Patton was disappointing, because he was nothing like he appeared to be-- much smaller person. And he looked-- he looked bigger, but he really wasn't. He was a small man and not very impressive, other than all his paraphernalia-- this silver handled pistol-- or his pearl handled pistols and stuff like that. He was-- but he was a hell of a soldier. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't know much about what camp were.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't. Until-- when we were briefed, we were not told what to expect. Our job was to take-- to fly these men. And we used L5-- the picture of that L5-- because you could land that airplane anyplace. I could land it out here in the street. You needed about 600 feet with a full load-- a passenger and a full load of fuel. And it was designed for that purpose.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many passengers--

speaker: interviewee
text: One.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did that take?

speaker: interviewee
text: One passenger-- pilot and one passenger, which is why they had-- our flights were usually three to five, seven, depending on-- OK, so we were briefed. We're told this is what we're going to do. And you know, we-- OK-- kind of a come down or let down for us from flying fighter planes to-- to what we call grasshoppers. And then we were briefed again, that OK, the first-- the first place we're going to go to was Buchenwald, which was-- what was the name of the-- the government was there. The old German government was there. Oh, damn, it's on my sheet.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the Weimar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Weimar, that's it. And we had good charts and maps. And of course our flight leader was our navigator, so we mainly just followed him. We flew into Weimar, and we landed on the road and still didn't know the name of the camp. Someone--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that? What date?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in-- this was April-- about the middle of April-- the 14th, 15th, around in there. The camp was liberated, I think, on the 12th of April and the armored-- by an armored outfit. And they were still fighting. I mean, there were still a lot of combat in there. So these guys that liberated the camp were there for two days, and then they went on, and another group of infantry came in and took over the camp and eventually, by the time we got there, the tankers were gone, with few exceptions. There were still men-- the tankers were still in the towers. But-- and they had military government people. This guy-- the first one I met-- was a Jewish man who was from G-2, which was intelligence. And he spoke several languages. And he had been sent to kind of-- he was a second lieutenant-- to kind of get things organized, because it was very chaotic. They hadn't done anything to the camp by the time we got there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we got there, we landed. Command cars came out, got these officers, and they went into the camp with high-ranking other officers-- people who had been involved in the liberation-- colonels, brigadier generals, high-ranking officers. Now, the pilots were pretty much left to their own devices. So what we would do would be to find some staff sergeant with a jeep and say, hey, take us into the camp. What is all this? And so he would explain to us, this is Buchenwald concentration camp, a Nazi concentration camp where they kept all the Jews and the gypsies and the criminals and what have you. He didn't know any more about it-- much more about it than we did. So, we would-- we would-- you could-- but here's the thing that I want to emphasize about this. We could smell this camp-- this is Buchenwald now-- from about a mile away, from about where we landed. You could smell something very disagreeable. And I asked the sergeant when I-- the guy that I connected up with was a Signal Corps guy who had been in the camp and taken pictures and was running around just trying to help people like me see what was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we're driving toward the entrance to the camp, and all of these camps from a distance looked the same. They all had the same kind of gates. They all had the same towers on concrete or brick towers on each side. They all had enclosures of barbed wire and electric fences. They all had moats of one kind or another. Some of them were out in the open. Some of them were in semi-wooded areas. There were usually-- inside of each of the main camps were a section that was all barbed wired so you couldn't get in or out at all with special prisoners in there, which were left-- they were starving them to death. If you committed some kind of-- there'd be 40, 50 men in there, no clothes no water, no food, no nothing, no shelter, no blankets, and they left them there till they died. And then they'd have to take the barbed wire out. And there probably would be-- half of them would be dead. I mean, I saw this at Buchenwald and also at Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find any alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, inside the enclosure, yeah. Yeah, there were some-- they had already started to dismantle. When this sergeant showed this to me, he explained to me what this was. Now, he'd been there for at least a day, so he knew pretty much where everything was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had-- there were still dead people in there, but they had taken the ones that were alive. But they all died. And-- but they had-- all they had them was cut enough wire to get a couple of guys in there to get them out. They left it. They were instructed to leave things pretty much as they were. Don't change anything until these people from SHAEF decide. What is SHAEF?

speaker: interviewee
text: Supreme-- Supreme Headquarters Allied Expeditionary Forces. That's what Eisenhower's-- that was what-- he was the supreme commander, and SHAEF was his organization. Supreme-- whatever I said-- Supreme Headquarters Allied Expeditionary Forces.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this driver said-- first-- first he took me to this enclosure where these men had-- and he said there were 40, 50 guys in there, no clothes, no water, no food no blankets, no nothing. I said, what? And they were dead, these guys that were still there, probably 10 or 15 had expired. We're going to stop this tape now.
